notable.notable_blurb: Maryland suffrage leader and social reformer

institutions.institution_blurb: Among the first women at Johns Hopkins Medical School; left to marry and pursue reform.

cross_connections.display_label: was also a prominent suffrage leader who married a Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Took suffrage to the streets by automobile
narrative_blocks.body: She founded Maryland's Just Government League in 1909 and its weekly Maryland Suffrage News in 1912, then carried the cause across the state in open-air meetings held from an automobile. In 1917 she became editor of The Suffragist, the National Woman's Party paper.

narrative_blocks.header: Her sister was Katharine Hepburn's mother
narrative_blocks.body: Edith and her older sister Katharine Houghton Hepburn were both prominent leaders of the woman-suffrage movement; that sister's daughter was the actress Katharine Hepburn, making Edith the actress's aunt. The Houghton sisters were raised by freethinking parents who championed women's education.

narrative_blocks.header: Left Johns Hopkins medicine for a reformer's life
narrative_blocks.body: One of the first women admitted to the Johns Hopkins School of Medicine, she left without finishing to marry her classmate Donald Hooker. With him she founded the Guild of St. George, a Baltimore home for unwed mothers and their children.

burial.plot_notes: Cremated; ashes interred at Evergreen Cemetery, New Haven.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended Hopkins Grammar School, New Haven.
M.D. 1905; later associate professor of physiology.

cross_connections.display_label: was an earlier Yale physician famed for medical ethics
devised the Ames medium for living brain tissue

narrative_blocks.header: His electric shocks could restart a stopped heart
narrative_blocks.body: Working at Johns Hopkins in the early 1930s, he showed that a properly graduated electric shock could restore the rhythm of a fibrillating heart — the experimental basis of modern cardiac defibrillation. Alone or with collaborators he published more than forty papers on the circulation.

narrative_blocks.header: Edited the physiology journal thirty-two years, mostly unpaid
narrative_blocks.body: He served as managing editor of the American Journal of Physiology for thirty-two years, much of it without pay. He made the journal self-supporting and in 1921 helped found its companion, Physiological Reviews.

military_service.unit: Johns Hopkins Hospital Unit 118, Army Medical Corps

narrative_blocks.header: Operated on Japanese prisoners and wounded Americans alike
narrative_blocks.body: A Johns Hopkins-trained surgeon, he served nearly four years with the hospital's Unit 118 in the Pacific Theater during World War II, operating in field hospitals in New Guinea and the Philippines on Japanese prisoners as well as wounded Americans. He was discharged a major in 1946 and became a thoracic surgeon in Baltimore.

narrative_blocks.header: First cousin of the actress Katharine Hepburn
narrative_blocks.body: His mother, Edith Houghton Hooker, was the eldest of the Houghton sisters; the youngest, Katharine, was the mother of the actress Katharine Hepburn, making him the actress's first cousin.

narrative_blocks.header: Rode a motorcycle to Beirut to teach English
narrative_blocks.body: After Princeton he taught English at a boarding school in Beirut, arriving on a motorcycle he had bought in Europe and ridden across Turkey and Syria. He returned to Baltimore for a career in commercial and investment banking, retiring as a principal at the firm Alex Brown and Sons.

narrative_blocks.header: FBI speechwriter who chronicled five intertwined families
narrative_blocks.body: She earned a doctorate from George Mason and worked at the FBI, first as a speechwriter for the director and later as a unit chief in international operations. She wrote a three-hundred-page history of the Houghton, Hooker, Hoyt, Creighton and related families, now held in the Library of Congress.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1972 psychedelic album found fame decades later
narrative_blocks.body: He fronted the D.R. Hooker Band and self-released the album 'The Truth' in 1972, recorded with local Connecticut musicians after only a few rehearsals. Largely unknown in his lifetime, it became a sought-after private-press classic among psychedelic-music collectors and was later reissued.

INST146.name: Johns Hopkins University
INST146.notes:
Founded 1876 Baltimore MD with a $7 million bequest from Quaker entrepreneur Johns Hopkins. The first American research university on the German model — pioneering the graduate doctoral program. Schools of particular tree relevance: School of Medicine (1893, with attached Hospital), Krieger School of Arts and Sciences, Whiting School of Engineering.

HOOKER-TREE CONNECTIONS — 11 across Hooker and Talcott branches, concentrated in medicine and graduate education:

MEDICINE:
- HD04886 Dr. Sarah Hooker Knutti (Hopkins School of Medicine)
- I00334 Doctor James Shepherd (Hopkins Hospital)
- HD1183 Doctor George Burton Gilbert
- I00276 Agnes Boxley Newton Hollerith

BALTIMORE FAMILY CLUSTER (overlaps with Brown Memorial Presbyterian INST147):
- I01138 Bayard Turnbull (Baltimore architect)
- I01141 Jerome Herrick Tyson 'Jerry' Kidder (Baltimore Gas & Electric)

WOOLSEY FAMILY CLUSTER:
- HD1236 Alexander Hamilton 'Sandy' Woods Woolsey
- HD2073 John Mumford Woolsey (Federal Judge — Ulysses obscenity trial 1933)

OTHERS:

-
- X02091 Jean Alexandra McCain Morgan

Hopkins's Baltimore location made it the natural medical and graduate destination for the Jones-Sparhawk Baltimore family cluster centered around Brown Memorial Presbyterian Church (INST147) — TD0240 Rev. John Sparhawk Jones's founding pastorate 1870+.
